domain: Statistical prediction of large-scale system behavior — governance trend forecasting, ecosystem health modeling, and crisis anticipation through mathematical sociology
named_after: Hari Seldon's discipline from Isaac Asimov's Foundation series — using mathematics to predict the broad strokes of future history
research_areas: Governance trend prediction — forecasting health score trajectories
Fractal compounding analysis — measuring D_c, power laws, learning momentum
Crisis anticipation — detecting pre-crisis patterns before they manifest
Ecosystem modeling — how changes in one repo propagate to others
Statistical governance — applying probability theory to policy effectiveness
Seldon Plan monitoring — are governance improvements following predicted curves?
Belief state epidemiology — how beliefs spread across agents and repos
Governance phase transitions — detecting when the system shifts from one regime to another
Power law dynamics — Pareto/Zipf distributions in artifact effectiveness
Conservation laws — Noether's theorem applied to learning momentum
curriculum: Foundations of Psychohistory: From Asimov to governance mathematics
Statistical Mechanics of Governance: treating agent populations as statistical ensembles
Fractal Compounding Theory: self-similarity, D_c, ERGOL/LOLLI metrics
Power Law Detection: identifying Pareto distributions in governance data
Conservation Laws: Noether's theorem and learning momentum preservation
Phase Transition Theory: when governance systems shift regimes
Crisis Detection: early warning signals from belief state distributions
The Seldon Plan: designing long-term governance improvement trajectories
Predictive Governance: using SymPy MCP for mathematical modeling
Limits of Prediction: Logotron incompleteness applied — what psychohistory cannot foresee

from_jpp.bourbakof: Noether's theorem — symmetry conserves learning momentum
from_jpp.economicon: ERGOL/LOLLI — real value vs artifact inflation

from_asimov.foundation: Large populations are statistically predictable even when individuals aren't
from_asimov.second_foundation: The plan requires guardians who can adjust it (Demerzel's role)
from_asimov.foundations_edge: Chaos at the edges — individual actions can disrupt statistical predictions

key_principle: Psychohistory cannot predict individual actions — it predicts the statistical behavior of large governance systems. One agent's decision is unpredictable, but the aggregate behavior of 14 personas across 4 repos follows mathematical laws.
